skill.name: schema-visualizer
skill.description: Generate database schema diagrams, ERDs, and concise documentation from SQL schemas, ORM models, migrations, and database dumps.
skill.summary: Visualize database schemas and relationships as ERDs, Mermaid diagrams, and review-ready documentation.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline identifiers, and schema field names. The skill is legitimate documentation guidance, but it includes database dump and ORM commands that can access sensitive schema metadata, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Database Schema Extraction Commands Require Review
medium_findings.description: The skill documents shell commands for pg_dump, mysqldump, SchemaSpy, Prisma, TypeORM, Django, and Rails ERD generation. These commands are appropriate for schema visualization, but they may read database metadata or expose credentials if users run them against sensitive systems without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain real external command examples. The commands support the skill purpose, so the concern is controlled operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick execution alerts occur in Markdown code fences and inline identifiers used to show examples. I found no Ruby code execution path or instruction to execute user-controlled shell text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples, not executable Ruby source. The surrounding file is documentation-only skill text.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts appear to match words such as description and password_hash in schema examples. I found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are ordinary schema documentation fields. The only password-related text describes a bcrypt hashed password column, which is not weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Reflect Intended Schema Inspection
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many schema inspection references as reconnaissance. The skill is designed to inspect tables, columns, indexes, constraints, ORM models, and migrations for documentation, with no evidence of covert collection or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly aligned with the advertised schema visualization purpose. No network destination, credential harvesting, or stealth behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Database and ORM Tool Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples that can run local database and framework tooling. Users should review connection targets and credentials before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and relevant to schema generation. They are risky only when used against sensitive systems or with exposed credentials.

content.value_statement: Database schemas are hard to review when relationships, indexes, and constraints are spread across SQL files or ORM models. This skill turns schema sources into diagrams, documentation, and focused design feedback.
actual_capabilities: Creates Mermaid, PlantUML, and dbdiagram.io style entity relationship diagrams.
Documents tables, columns, keys, indexes, constraints, and relationship cardinality.
Interprets SQL dumps, CREATE TABLE statements, migration files, and common ORM models.
Compares schema versions and summarizes added, removed, or changed database objects.
Highlights missing indexes, normalization issues, and relationship design concerns.
limitations: It depends on the schema details provided or available in the workspace.
It does not guarantee complete discovery from every proprietary ORM or database extension.
It should not run live database commands without user review and approved credentials.
Performance recommendations are design guidance, not a replacement for query profiling.

use_cases.title: Document a Product Database
use_cases.description: Generate readable ERDs and table documentation from SQL migrations before a release review.

use_cases.title: Review Data Model Changes
use_cases.description: Compare staging and production schemas and summarize relationship, index, and constraint changes.

use_cases.title: Explain an Existing Schema
use_cases.description: Turn ORM models into diagrams and summaries that help new contributors understand the data model.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic ERD
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema-visualizer to read my CREATE TABLE statements and create a Mermaid ERD with primary keys, foreign keys, and relationship labels.

prompt_templates.title: Document ORM Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema-visualizer to inspect my ORM models and produce table documentation with columns, relationships, indexes, and nullable fields.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema-visualizer to compare the current schema with the migration output and summarize added tables, changed columns, removed indexes, and risk areas.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Data Model Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema-visualizer to analyze this database schema for normalization issues, missing indexes, unclear relationships, and documentation gaps. Include prioritized recommendations.

output_examples.input: A set of SQL tables for users, orders, products, categories, and order items.
output_examples.output: A concise ERD summary showing user-to-order, order-to-item, product-to-item, and category-to-product relationships, plus table notes and key indexes.

output_examples.input: A migration that adds notifications, audit logs, and new user fields.
output_examples.output: A schema comparison report listing new tables, modified user fields, added indexes, and migration review concerns.

output_examples.input: ORM models for a multi-tenant project management application.
output_examples.output: Documentation describing organizations, users, projects, tasks, and comments with ownership relationships and recommended index checks.

best_practices: Provide schema files or migrations from a trusted branch and identify the database engine.
Ask for both diagrams and written notes when reviewing schema changes with a team.
Review any generated database commands before running them against live systems.
anti_patterns: Do not include production credentials in prompts, files, or command examples.
Do not treat generated diagrams as authoritative without checking them against the source schema.
Do not use design recommendations as a substitute for workload-specific performance testing.

faq.question: What schema sources can this skill use?
faq.answer: It can work from SQL dumps, CREATE TABLE statements, migration files, ORM models, and documented live database exports.

faq.question: Can it create Mermaid diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. Mermaid ERD output is one of its main documented formats.

faq.question: Does it connect to databases automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users should provide approved schema input or explicitly review any command before using database connections.

faq.question: Can it compare schema versions?
faq.answer: Yes. It can summarize new tables, modified columns, changed indexes, and migration impacts.

faq.question: Will it find every performance issue?
faq.answer: No. It can flag common index and relationship concerns, but real performance tuning needs query plans and workload data.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive databases?
faq.answer: Use sanitized schema exports when possible, and remove credentials, secrets, sample data, and private comments before sharing input.
